sentences.text: No, I, I gave it to, to, to Dan, which I'm very sorry.
But, you know, about that, the one woman, I have a picture book from, from--
Yes, 1944, like I said, it was the uprising ghetto sonderkommando.
The Hungarian transport stopped, but the killing didn't.
The killing went on in different ways, not massive, you know, transports with gassing.
And they killed the people by shooting them in Auschwitz or in the woods there.
And we went on doing regular our job, I mean, the 12 hour shift, you know, sorting the clothing.
The SS came more and more stealing clothing, and jewelery, and valuables.

And we knew all because we found many times in, in their luggage newspapers with the newest, you know, what happened in the world, that the Russian's front is closer, the Allies already occupied quite almost, you know, all Europe, except Germany.
And so we always thought, you know, we-- maybe some of us will be so lucky to see the light, I would say.
But our hope was not too much, because the SS always assured us the only way is out through the chimney.
But you know how I said from the start, I wanted always to see a tomorrow.
Maybe tomorrow will be better.
And it was about, I think so, close to new year.
They-- we didn't have-- we came from the night shift.
And the day shift was already lined up outside.
And then we had to stop and line up.
And in front of the roll call was, were two nurses, and a table, and something they had in front.
And always, the first five in the row had to go through a table.
And they drew blood from each of us.
And we were wondering that they needed the Jewish blood.
They needed, one nurse said-- and then rumor went through the whole kommando that they need the blood for the wounded German soldiers.
So even before the end of the war, not [? only ?] they squeezed out the life, inch by inch of us, but they squeezed out from every one of us the blood too.
Then we got, you know, a whole piece of bread and some salami, a reward for having, you know, being blood donors.
It was not donors.
It was done by force.
So, this part-- I remember the first time I had the interview at the [?
Oral ?] History Project-- [?
Lonna Silver-- ?] and I forgot it.
And then I went back to her, and we made a two hour tape again about, you know, what happened later, because many thing I forgot, the blood taking from the whole kommando, 600 girls.
So it was December already, and they were losing.
But they still squeezed out not only the life, but the blood of us too.
So I'm just saying what cruelty, through the last minute, went on.
Even the [? mass-- ?] whatever they could do.
So that was.
And then, you know, the hanging in January of the four girls who smuggled the gun powder.
And then, you know, the, the, the feeling and the scariness that now they are to end-- when it's our turn, when they are going to kill us.
You know how it goes.
But then the 18th of January, 1945, we had to line up again.
And the whole Auschwitz complex was evacuated, everything.
You know, Auschwitz had many side camps-- Buna, Monowitz, beside Birkenau, [NON-ENGLISH], Auschwitz.
Many, I don't know, momentarily, all of the names.
But the whole complex-- I don't know, at peacetime in January, 1945, how many thousands, and now hundreds of thousands prisoners.
[SIGHS] It was very cold.
Snow was about over a meter or two meters high.
And we had to march to the mountains, Carpathian mountains, not where the civilian population was.
The first who marched, they paved the road for the rest.
The SS was on the sides, with the ox [? leg ?] usually.
Some of horses were too-- on horses, I would say.
But when we left, we who were in the special kommando, we were well fed.
And we put on clothing, because we could still grab for pants, and underwear, and warm things.
But those from the camp had only those very thin summery clothing, wooden clogs.
I had already shoes.
And each shoe was different.
It doesn't matter, but they were shoes and warm socks.
And when we walked, you know, who was not able to walk was shot right away.
We walked way behind our kommando in the back of the long lines of prisoners, so we walked over corpses.
If the corpse has still something usable, a shoe or a sweater, we took it.
But we didn't have strength to pull them up to the side.
So, you know, it was stepped in in the snow.
The road were paved with corpses, but we walked about a week towards Berlin, because the part around Berlin, they needed still us to clean the airfields, to clean, you know, the bombarded cities.
The only thing but we had to eat was snow.
Frozen, wet, slept-- you know, came, came the night, we slept on the snow.
You know, you get wet, you huddle.
And then everything freezes on you.
Many had freezing noses, frozen toes.
I didn't dare take off my shoes, wet shoes, because I wouldn't be able to put them back.
The, the stockings were wet, everything was wet.
Finally, we came to rest in one farm, big farm, where it was straw.
So we rested there for a while, then we continued until we came to a city-- to a, to, to a train station, which was Loslau.
I don't know how the city is now named because it belongs to Poland.
It was a part of Poland.
That was already the border of Germany.
Then they loaded us in open coal, coal carriages, or how you call  it, coal wagons-- open.
And it was snowing and winter.
Some of the girls could go to the engine who pulled the train, and hot water was dropping.
Other was nothing.
They squeezed 100 of us in one open cattle car.
[SIGHS] We had room only to stand, and freezing.
Many of the inmates died there, so we just throw them out.
Naturally, took everything which was usable away from them.
I don't know how long we traveled.
I can't tell you.
But the hunger-- you don't know, who was not in a situation, how hunger hurts.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] It's worse than a disease.
Hunger hurts very much.

This time I promised myself, if I ever survive, and I get home, and I live a normal life, and maybe I get once married, and I have a family, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they never, ever will know what hunger means.
[SOBBING] I didn't dream of too much, only to have a roof and to have what to eat.
But this, this journey on the train from Loslau to Ravensbruck, that was the worst experience what I ever had, freezing to death, throwing over my dead comrades who survived three years in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And finally we arrived about to Ravensbruck.
That was a women's camp, which from where we saw in Auschwitz the first day when we came, the thousand inmates from Ravensbruck.
And the camp was full.
We didn't have room where even to sit outside because that was, you know, the only camps which were still in the German Reich.
The rest were already liberated.
This part was liberated by the Russian armies, where we came.
And it was so full.
We didn't have, you know, a little space well.
But they built a big tent there, where thousands and thousands of inmates from camps from Poland and Germany came there where it drove us.
Still, you know, they took us with them, the SS.
And my friends with whom we survived and I were sitting outside the tent.
We thought that was the last minutes of our lives.
No food, no nothing.
And still, other inmates were pouring in.
So then I, I think we squeezed in somewhere to the tent.
It was, you know, full of mud.
But we were sitting on the floor anyway.
No windows there then, just the tent.
And then we got, I think-- so one day, a day later, we had to line up.
And they brought kettles.
But we were pushing so hard to these kettles, the kettles turned over.
But in front of the tent was the frozen, I mean, soil.
And it was like glass, shiny.
The food poured outside, and the food was gone.
You know what we did?
We were laying down and licking the food from the, from [SOBBING] the ice.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So we got some.
But later, about two, three days later, again some trucks came in.
And we were loaded on trucks, not too many, but maybe 1,000.
And we traveled, also not far away from Ravensbruck, to another camp.
It's called Jugendlager.
And we all got new numbers who went on that on a pink slip.
And we came to this Jugendlager.
This was not far away, also, from where Jugendlager where Hitlerjugend was trained.
You remember the Hitlerjugend?
They prepared them for, for being good soldiers.
So, so they left from there.
And we were brought there, around a thousand.
But they locked us in in the barrack.
It was one huge, open barrack, you know.
And we were sitting on the floor.
But again, we heard that they are going to put in gas, gasses.
So we started breaking windows and we run out.
Only recently-- I don't know where I was or I read that the pink slips what we get, it meant all, you know, a certificate to death, that those who were taken had to be sentenced already to death.
But since we broke all the windows and started running out, and it was in woods, this Jugendlager was, you know, in woods, so we started running out.
They couldn't get us together.
So, but again, we got, once a day, some soap.
I think so, I managed to get some.
And then we were loaded again on trucks.
But there was one SS who was a woman, who was an SS woman in Birkenau too.
And some of the girls recognized her.
And she told them that they shouldn't worry, because we were afraid to go up to, to the tracks again.
But, you know, they were pushing us, the SS with the dogs.
We had to go up the tracks, I mean, pushed up the tracks.
So we came to-- but she said that we are going to clean some airfield.
And indeed, when we came to this camp, it was Rechlin, Retzow.
It was a huge airfield there, and the German's airplanes were still starting, and bombarded the Allies around there, Berlin.
We had to clean the holes, the airfield.
When it was an attack and the bombs were falling, we had to stay outside and clean.
But the SS disappeared.
And it went on there-- we were there about beginning of March, or a little later.
And we were there about a month doing this job.
And then we had to walk again, a march toward Berlin.
So we walked, but we saw already on, on the roads all kinds of civilians.
One road went up, one road down, the [POLISH] flying over our heads, you know, constantly.
Noise, and bombardment, and, and planes seeing, so we know.
But you know what happened, too?
Some airplanes were, were throwing packages on us.
But who caught the package?
You know, mostly the SS still was there, caught the packages.
They took everything.
What I got was a big piece of ivory soap.
Marching, no food, no nothing.
We marched again, I don't know how long, until we came to Neubrandenburg.
Neubrandenburg, again, some worked still.
There was a factory in a town [? in the ?] [? hill ?], close by.
I don't know it was direct in Neubrandenburg.
So was there-- and end of April, too, you heard all what's going on, bombardment, and, and, you know, Allied forces, and Russian.
So [SIGHS] we got up in the morning.
We were still there, you know, separated.
It was not at the city of Neubrandenburg, outside somewhere.
And from the factories, the girls came out, and no SS-- end of April.
So we just walked around.
Eventually, it was the 4th, 5th May, we saw tanks coming.
But the tanks had stars.
We knew the tanks, that American has stars.
And we didn't see ever any of them, but stars.
And finally, Russian tanks came in and liberated us.
We were very happy, the 5th of May.
But, you know, then the Russians were very nice to us.
I have [? no ?] thing.
They brought us clothing.
They made a field kitchen.
They didn't have, themselves, too much.
And then they took us to Neubrandenburg.
But many people, you know, were running out to the city.
And we saw many prisoners on the road died because the food started to-- you know, there, Neubrandenburg, was a [POLISH] place where the Germans had lots of food stored.
So broke in, and, you know, when you're so hungry you eat everything, but your body rejects that.
So, many died after eating.
I, I, I person and my friend didn't so much, but we were displaced in the army barracks for quarantine.
Russian doctors came and they examined us.
And, and, you know, we had food brought.
We could shower there, because they had water.
The army barrack's well equipped.
And we were well-fed.
And we were there one month.
And then an international commission came there from, you know, the West and from the East and the Russians.
And they told us, since we are citizens [SOBBING] of the world-- because we didn't have papers, nothing, and they were citizens of all Europe, yes-- so we can choose and go wherever you want to.
Me and my friends, we were so anxious just to go back home.
So we didn't want to go with them at all.
We went by foot.
Can you imagine, from Berlin all the way to Prague?
And we, you know, I remember, I don't know which city it was, we said-- you know, there was a Russian soldier, and we said, we need some food.
And he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], come girls.
And he took us to a [? won ?] house.
And he said, I'm going to stay here.
You go in and take whatever you want to.
So, you can imagine, we went in.
But you know, it was also some farmhouse or something.
And probably the owners who were there saw coming a Russian soldier accompanying the hordes of, of, you know, former concentration camp inmates, because we had still our shaved heads.
So they escaped.
We came in through the house.
On the table was a table cloth with still hot soup.
And you know what?
We were, this time, maybe seven, eight, identical exactly.
And we looked at this table, and we couldn't touch it.
We couldn't.
Can you imagine three years being in a concentration camp, deprived of everything, and see a normal house and a normal table?
But [?
Shari, ?] I remembering this, she got such [POLISH], angry, she flipped the tablecloth and broke everything.
And then we started to rummage the house, to look in the closets.
And we found a woman hiding there.
She pulled her out by her hair.
And she said to her, look what you did to me.
And she was screaming, [GERMAN].
And you know, the anger in us that she's Nazi, now she slapped her quite a few times.
But we searched, and what do you think what we found?
Two SS uniforms.
So, can you imagine, with the two SS uniforms, what anger broke out in us?
We all slapped her, but we didn't touch the food because we were afraid she poisoned it.
And we didn't kill her.
We didn't kill her, but we tore everything in this house through the window.
Even the feather bed we emptied out.
But we found in the cellar full of food, sugar, and flours, and everything.
We took what we could, and the rest we destroyed.
And we left, marching toward our country.
Oh, I don't know, a week, two.
I don't know.
But you know what, we had little packages, what we stole there, naturally.
We changed our clothes for warmer, shoes what we found, and what we couldn't and we thought we could use, food, in little packages.
So, on the road, a truck stopped.
And the Russian soldier said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
He took us on the-- he gave us a lift.
But what do you think?
When we stopped, everything what we had, they took away from us.
But I understand now-- not this time-- they were fighting years and years.
They needed, probably, that too.
I don't, you know-- first of all, I was very angry, this time.
But later, when you live a normal life, and you read about what happened, deprived those of everything too of their families, so I understand now.
But again, we were there just with our clothing.
Because the transportation was destroyed-- no trains, no nothing.
It was bombarded, everything.
We saw bombarded city.
People, you know, the German people wander and go over back and forth.
It was nothing there.
How could we, how could we have transportation if it was none?
And still the armies, you know-- only the Russian army.
This part was all, you know, occupied by the Russian, you know, armies.
Then we came to Dresden.
You know how Dresden was destroyed.
We went all around Dresden.
So it was all destroyed that we couldn't.
And besides this, after we had experience, many probably the Russians who went with trucks, would take us.
But we were afraid because they took everything from us.
